intro: Apidog and Azure OpenAI Service bill differently, which matters more than feature parity here — Apidog offers a free tier, paid from $672/YEAR, while Azure OpenAI Service sells a subscription from $1 with a free tier. Apidog is all-in-one API design, mocking, testing, and documentation tool. Azure OpenAI Service is Azure-hosted OpenAI models with enterprise compliance and regional endpoints. Pick Apidog when the job is api gateway and rate limiting and you accept medium lock-in; pick Azure OpenAI Service when it is api gateway and rate limiting and you accept medium lock-in. The data we have shows Apidog at mostly transparent pricing and docs with a polished developer experience, and Azure OpenAI Service at mostly transparent pricing and docs with solid developer experience. The honest trade-off: neither is universal — Apidog is a poor fit for internal, simple projects, and Azure OpenAI Service is a poor fit for internal, simple projects. Match the pricing model and lock-in level to how your team actually works, not the feature list.
quick_take: Apidog is for all-in-one API design; Azure OpenAI Service is for Azure-hosted OpenAI models; decide based on pricing model fit.
verdict_a: Choose Apidog if a free tier with paid upgrade from $672/YEAR is the right shape and the work maps to all-in-one API design, mocking, testing, and documentation tool.
verdict_b: Choose Azure OpenAI Service if solid but not perfect DX is acceptable and the work lines up with Azure-hosted OpenAI models with enterprise compliance and regional endpoints.

faq.q: Is Apidog cheaper than Azure OpenAI Service?
faq.a: Apidog starts at $672/YEAR on a freemium model; Azure OpenAI Service starts at $1 on a subscription model. That makes the sticker comparison depend on usage — a low entry price can get expensive once volume ramps, so price at your actual workload.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Apidog to Azure OpenAI Service?
faq.a: Migration is possible in either direction. Apidog carries medium lock-in and Azure OpenAI Service carries medium lock-in, so plan for meaningful but not blocking rework. Run both in parallel before you fully cut over.

Apidog scores higher on developer experience in our data (5/5 vs 4/5). That reflects public feedback on docs, onboarding, and day-to-day ergonomics. Still, run a spike with your own code — DX ratings don't capture every edge.

faq.q: Is Azure OpenAI Service a good alternative to Apidog?
faq.a: Yes — Azure OpenAI Service is a reasonable alternative to Apidog for api gateway and rate limiting. The practical differences are freemium-vs-subscription billing and medium-vs-medium lock-in. If those fit your constraints better, treat Azure OpenAI Service as a credible swap.
